title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
author: James Silberman
orientation: Investigative report on how Indiana pro-life politicians and Right to Life lobbyists killed HB1430, the Protection at Conception Act, by refusing to grant it a committee hearing despite its non-severability clause refuting their stated objections.
audiences: Indiana voters and constituents of Smaltz, Bosma, and Nisly
Pastors and Christians weighing pro-life vs. abolitionist strategy
Constitutional originalists interested in nullification and judicial supremacy
Journalists and political historians documenting the abolition movement

questions.question: Why did HB1430 fail to receive a hearing in the Indiana legislature?
questions.answer: House Committee on Public Policy Chairman Ben Smaltz declined to schedule the bill before the February 18th deadline for first hearings, killing it without debate.
questions.quote: Indiana House Committee on Public Policy Chairman Ben Smaltz (R-Auburn) decided against hearing the bill as the deadline for first hearings passed on February 18th.

questions.question: What would HB1430 have actually done if passed and signed?
questions.audience: Indiana voters and constituents of Smaltz, Bosma, and Nisly
questions.answer: It would have defined human physical life as beginning at conception, asserted Indiana's Ninth and Tenth Amendment authority to protect that life, nullified federal interference, and completely abolished abortion in the state.
questions.quote: HB1430, authored by State Rep. Curt Nisly (R-Goshen), would have added an article to Indiana criminal codes specifying that human physical life begins at conception, asserted the state’s authority to protect human physical life grounded in the ninth and tenth amendment of the US Constitution along with the Declaration of Independence, and established that any attempt to interfere with Indiana’s protection of human physical life is legally null.

questions.question: What was Smaltz's stated objection to the Protection at Conception Act?
questions.audience: Pastors and Christians weighing pro-life vs. abolitionist strategy
questions.answer: Smaltz claimed in 2017 that passing the bill would be 'catastrophic to the pro-life movement' because a hostile judge could strike down the abolition sections while keeping the regulation-repeal sections intact.
questions.quote: He told the Fort Wayne Journal Gazette then that passing the Protection at Conception Act would be “catastrophic to the [pro-life movement].”

questions.question: How does the bill's non-severability clause refute Smaltz's argument?
questions.audience: Constitutional originalists interested in nullification and judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Indiana law states that if any part of a non-severable statute is invalidated, the entire statute is void, meaning a court could not selectively kill the abolition portions while preserving the repeal of pro-life regulations.
questions.quote: According to Indiana law, “[i]f a statute contains a nonseverability provision and if any part of that statute is declared invalid, the whole statute is void.” HB1430 also had a nonseverability clause.

questions.question: Where is the misinformation about non-severability originating?
questions.answer: The same false talking point used by Oklahoma pro-life politicians is being deployed by Indiana lobbyists, traced back to state and county affiliates of National Right to Life, including Allen County Right to Life.
questions.quote: The same misinformation is being spread by Oklahoma pro-life politicians and lobbyists and it comes from the same source: National Right to Life’s state- and county-level affiliates.

questions.question: What did House Speaker Brian Bosma say when asked about the Nisly bill?
questions.audience: Indiana voters and constituents of Smaltz, Bosma, and Nisly
questions.answer: Bosma rejected the Nisly bill at a press conference, saying he would only support a 'life bill' that he believed had 'Constitutional legs.'
questions.quote: Bosma: No. I’m talking about one that has some Constitutional legs.

questions.question: How does the article answer the constitutional objection raised by Bosma?
questions.audience: Constitutional originalists interested in nullification and judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Silberman argues Bosma's oath is to the Constitution rather than the Supreme Court, that Article VI only binds states to laws made pursuant to the Constitution, and that Jefferson and Madison endorsed state-level nullification of unconstitutional federal acts.
questions.quote: Article VI of the Constitution establishes that only those made pursuant to the Constitution are legitimate laws and binding on the states.

questions.question: What did Thomas Jefferson warn about treating judges as ultimate arbiters of constitutional questions?
questions.audience: Constitutional originalists interested in nullification and judicial supremacy
questions.answer: In his 1820 letter to Charles Jarvis, Jefferson called judicial supremacy 'a very dangerous doctrine' that would place the country under an oligarchic despotism, since judges share the same passions for party and power as other men.
questions.quote: You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy.

questions.question: What does the article cite to argue Roe v. Wade is not legitimate constitutional law?
questions.audience: Pastors and Christians weighing pro-life vs. abolitionist strategy
questions.answer: It quotes former Stanford Law dean John Hart Ely, who acknowledged Roe is not constitutional law and shows no obligation even to try to be.
questions.quote: [Roe v Wade] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be.

questions.question: How does the article frame the moral obligation to defy unjust judicial rulings?
questions.audience: Pastors and Christians weighing pro-life vs. abolitionist strategy
questions.answer: It argues defiance is the only suitable response when a higher authority commands an egregious act, and that 'just following orders from D.C.' cannot justify allowing mass murder.
questions.quote: “I’m just following orders from D.C.” is no excuse for allowing mass murder.

questions.question: Did Indiana Right to Life endorse Smaltz's primary challenger who supported abolition?
questions.audience: Indiana voters and constituents of Smaltz, Bosma, and Nisly
questions.answer: No. Indiana Right to Life gave Smaltz an unqualified endorsement in 2018 over William Carlin, despite Smaltz publicly supporting the legality of killing babies conceived in rape or incest.
questions.quote: Indiana Right to Life endorsed Smaltz in 2018 over challenger William Carlin, who ran on a platform of supporting the Protection at Conception Act. Their endorsement was unqualified despite Smaltz’s public support for the legality of murdering babies conceived in rape or incest.

questions.question: What is Hoosiers for Life planning in response to the bill being killed?
questions.audience: Journalists and political historians documenting the abolition movement
questions.answer: President Amy Schlicter said Hoosiers for Life intends to recruit primary challengers against Smaltz and other state-level Republican leaders in 2020 and will keep doing so until the legislature contains people who will stand for all life without exception.
questions.quote: “Until there are people in [the legislature] that will stand for life, all life, without exception, we will continue to find people to run in their districts.”

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Directly referenced in the opening of this article as the Oklahoma parallel to Smaltz and Bosma killing abolition bills.

related.title: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
related.reason: Continuation of Curt Nisly's effort to introduce a Protection at Conception Act in Indiana, with new co-author John Jacob.

related.title: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep
related.reason: Documents the abolitionist primary-challenger strategy that Hoosiers for Life promised at the end of this article.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Explains the broader abolitionist critique of the regulationist pro-life strategy that motivated Right to Life's opposition to HB1430.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Parallel example of pro-life politicians invoking judicial supremacy to reject state-level nullification of Roe, the same constitutional dodge Bosma used.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the same argument advanced here that pro-life judicial supremacy is incompatible with abolishing abortion.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Foundational case for the immediatist approach embodied in HB1430 versus the incremental regulation strategy defended by Smaltz and Right to Life.
